schools.summary: Chrysostom asks why Paul adds a second epistle and why he begins with the mercies and consolation of God — having said in the first that he would come, and promised it again, yet now writing again, he opens with comfort to soothe and prepare them; Paul opening the second letter as an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God (not self-appointed but called, his authority divine), joining Timothy (a beloved fellow-worker, 'our brother'), and addressing the church of God at Corinth with all the saints in all Achaia — the apostolic greeting grounding his authority in God's will and embracing both the Corinthian church and the wider province.

themes: Paul, an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God
Timothy our brother
the church at Corinth, with all the saints in Achaia
the apostolic salutation

schools.summary: Gill: this salutation is the same as in the former epistle and common to all Paul's epistles; Ellicott and Robertson note its identity with the standard Pauline greeting (cf. Romans 1:7; 1 Corinthians 1:3); Paul pronouncing grace and peace from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ — the customary apostolic blessing (grace, God's free favour, the fountain of all blessing, and peace, the resulting tranquillity with God, conscience, and others, invoked upon the Corinthians), flowing jointly from the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ as one divine source (the deity of Christ implied in the joint naming).

themes: grace be to you and peace
from God our Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ
the customary apostolic blessing
grace and peace from the Father and the Son

schools.summary: Gill: God is blessed by men when they praise and glorify him; here he is described by his relation to Christ (the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ) and as the Father of mercies and the God of all comfort; Matthew Henry: after the preface, Paul begins the narrative of God's goodness to him and his fellow-labourers; Paul ascribing blessing to God — the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of mercies (the source and parent of all mercy), and the God of all comfort (the fountain of every consolation) — the praise that names God by his tender titles, so that whatever comfort the afflicted receive flows from him as its spring, to encourage the afflicted.

themes: Blessed be God... the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ
the Father of mercies
the God of all comfort
God the source of every consolation

schools.summary: Gill: Paul confirms the character of God as the God of all comfort by his own experience — that, though in great tribulation and affliction for the sake of Christ and his Gospel, he and his fellow-ministers were not left destitute of divine help and support; Paul declaring that God comforts them in all tribulation, that they may comfort others by the comfort they themselves receive from God — the gracious economy of comfort (God's consolation in affliction not given for the sufferer alone but equipping him to console others in their troubles, the comforted becoming a channel of comfort, the experiential ministry fitting the comforted minister to comfort others).

themes: who comforteth us in all our tribulation
that we may be able to comfort them which are in any trouble
the comfort that overflows to others
the experiential ministry of consolation

schools.summary: Gill: 'the sufferings of Christ' are not those which he suffered in his own person for his people, but those which his members suffer for his sake, said to abound in them because of their variety and greatness; Paul declaring that, as the sufferings of Christ abound in them, so their consolation also abounds by Christ — the gracious proportion (the believer's sufferings for Christ, the afflictions endured for his sake, which Christ counts his own as the union of head and members, are matched and exceeded by the consolation that flows through Christ as its channel and source, so abounding suffering is met with abounding comfort, the believer never comfortless).

themes: the sufferings of Christ abound in us
our consolation also aboundeth by Christ
the gracious proportion
abounding suffering met with abounding comfort

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Paul lays down a second ground of comfort — that the salvation of the disciples was procured by the apostles' sufferings ('faint not, nor be confounded and afraid because we are afflicted; for this same thing were rather a reason for your being of good cheer'); Paul declaring that, whether afflicted or comforted, it is for the Corinthians' consolation and salvation — the selfless aim of the apostolic sufferings (Paul's afflictions, and the comfort he received, endured and granted not for himself but for the church's good, his trials confirming the gospel, his endurance teaching them to endure the same sufferings and strengthening them by his example).

themes: whether we be afflicted, it is for your consolation and salvation
effectual in the enduring of the same sufferings
whether we be comforted, it is for your consolation
the selfless aim of the apostolic sufferings

schools.summary: Gill: Paul had long entertained hopes that the work of God was begun upon their souls and would be carried on, and that they would hold on in the profession of their faith to the end, not moved by the afflictions, rejoicing in hope of the glory; Paul affirming that his hope of them is steadfast, knowing that as they are partakers of the sufferings, so they shall be also of the consolation — the confident assurance (Paul's hope firm, resting on the certainty that those who partake of Christ's sufferings shall as surely partake of his consolation, the fellowship of suffering guaranteeing the fellowship of comfort, grounded in God's work in them being carried on to the end, Philippians 1:6).

themes: our hope of you is stedfast
as ye are partakers of the sufferings, so... of the consolation
the fellowship of suffering and comfort
the confident assurance

schools.summary: Gill: Paul desired the Corinthians know of the trouble that lately befell them — partly to show his reason for thanksgiving, partly to encourage them to trust in God when in the utmost extremity; Paul telling the Corinthians of his trouble in Asia — pressed out of measure, above strength, so that he despaired even of life — the candid account of overwhelming affliction (Paul, in some severe and deadly peril in Asia, burdened beyond his own strength to the point of despairing of life itself, a trial so crushing that only God could sustain or deliver, candidly told not to hide his weakness but to magnify the deliverance and encourage their trust).

themes: our trouble which came to us in Asia
pressed out of measure, above strength
we despaired even of life
overwhelming affliction only God could sustain

schools.summary: Gill: by 'the sentence of death' is meant not any decree of God or sentence of condemnation by a magistrate, but a persuasion in their own breasts that they should die — so far were they from any hope of life that they looked upon themselves as dead men; Paul declaring that they had the sentence of death in themselves, that they should not trust in themselves but in God who raises the dead — the divine design in the extremity (God brought Paul to the very brink of death, where all self-confidence failed, precisely to wean him from trust in himself and cast him wholly on God, whose power to raise the dead is the ground of hope even in deadly peril).

themes: we had the sentence of death in ourselves
that we should not trust in ourselves
but in God which raiseth the dead
the trial weaning him from self-trust

schools.summary: Gill: being enabled to trust in God when all human hope and helps failed, to believe in hope against hope, the Lord appeared for them and delivered them from this heavy affliction, called a death because they were not only in danger of death but under its very sentence; Paul declaring that God delivered them from so great a death, does deliver, and in whom they trust will yet deliver — the threefold deliverance (past, present, and future), the confidence built on past mercy (God's deliverance from the deadly peril the ground of present and future trust, the experience of one rescue assuring the heart of rescues to come).

themes: who delivered us from so great a death
and doth deliver
he will yet deliver us
past mercy grounding present and future trust

schools.summary: Gill: though Paul ascribes the deliverance solely to God as its author and efficient cause, he takes notice of the prayers of the saints as helping causes or means of obtaining it — a very laudable practice, worthy of imitation, to pray for the ministers of the gospel, especially under affliction and persecution; Paul acknowledging that the Corinthians helped together by their prayers, that the gift (deliverance) obtained through many persons might issue in thanksgiving by many on his behalf — the value of intercessory prayer (a real means of Paul's deliverance, God the author yet prayer the means), the wide intercession producing wide thanksgiving (the many who prayed joining the many who give thanks).

themes: ye also helping together by prayer for us
the gift bestowed... by the means of many persons
thanks may be given by many
intercessory prayer a means of deliverance

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Paul opens yet another ground of comfort, exceeding great and well fitted to upraise a sinking mind — lest the hearer, having heard 'God comforted us' and 'God delivered us' and all ascribed to his mercies and their prayers, grow supine presuming only on God's mercy and others' prayers, he shows that they themselves contributed (by their integrity); Paul declaring that his rejoicing is the testimony of his conscience — that he had lived in simplicity (singleness of heart) and godly sincerity, not with fleshly wisdom (craft, cunning) but by the grace of God, in the world and especially toward the Corinthians — the clear conscience as a ground of joy (Paul's confidence resting not in self-merit but in the witness of a sincere life lived by grace, his integrity vindicated against the slanders of his foes).

themes: our rejoicing is this, the testimony of our conscience
in simplicity and godly sincerity
not with fleshly wisdom, but by the grace of God
the good conscience as a ground of joy

schools.summary: Barnes: Paul had declared he was actuated by pure intentions and entire sincerity, and now affirms that what he writes is consistent with what they read and acknowledge — no hidden or double meaning; Paul affirming that he writes nothing other than what they read or acknowledge, and trusting they will acknowledge it to the end — the transparency of his dealing (his letters carrying no hidden sense or double meaning, but saying plainly what they appear to say, matching his known character, so the Corinthians can fully acknowledge his sincerity, the consistency of word and life confirming his integrity, no craft or concealment).

themes: we write none other things... than what ye read or acknowledge
acknowledge even to the end
the transparency of Paul's dealing
no hidden or double meaning

schools.summary: Gill: they had acknowledged the apostle's integrity in part — some, a part of the church of Corinth, having acquitted him from the charge brought against him; and that, as he was their rejoicing, so they were his; Paul observing that they have acknowledged in part that he is their rejoicing, as they are his in the day of the Lord Jesus — the bond of mutual joy (Paul and the Corinthians, and shall be, each other's ground of glorying, the minister rejoicing in his converts and the converts in their minister, the converts the minister's joy and crown, 1 Thessalonians 2:19, a joy to be consummated at Christ's coming).

themes: ye have acknowledged us in part
we are your rejoicing, even as ye also are ours
in the day of the Lord Jesus
the mutual rejoicing of minister and converts

schools.summary: Gill: being fully persuaded of their affection for him (as having been instrumental in the conversion of many) and of their esteem, Paul was minded (desirous and determined, and so promised) to come to them; Matthew Henry: Paul here vindicates himself from the imputation of levity and inconstancy; Paul explaining that, in this confidence of their mutual love, he had purposed to come to them before, that they might have a second benefit — the loving intention behind the plan (his desire to visit twice, coming and returning, sprang from confidence in their affection and his wish to do them double good, not from fickleness, answering the charge that he was light and unstable).

themes: in this confidence I was minded to come unto you before
that ye might have a second benefit
the loving intention behind the plan
vindicating against the charge of levity

schools.summary: Gill: Paul's first intention was to come first to Corinth, then to Macedonia, taking this city in his way thither — an argument of his love and great desire to see them, since he might have gone a nearer way to Macedonia than by Corinth; Paul detailing his plan — to pass by them into Macedonia, return out of Macedonia to them, and be brought on his way toward Judaea by them — the careful itinerary that proves his affection (Paul had planned to visit Corinth both going and returning, the double visit a mark of love, choosing the longer route by them rather than the nearer way out of love, and counting on their help to forward him to Judaea, a mark of fellowship).

themes: to pass by you into Macedonia
to come again out of Macedonia unto you
brought on my way toward Judaea
the longer route chosen out of love

schools.summary: Gill: Paul asks whether, in determining to come and signifying it, he used lightness — acting rashly, unadvisedly, and without consideration, promising absolutely that he would come without annexing the condition 'if the Lord will' (James 4:15); Paul asking whether, in his change of plan, he used lightness or purposed according to the flesh, so that his yea and nay were unstable (saying yes and no in the same breath) — the rebuttal of the slander (his altering his travel plan was not levity or fleshly fickleness but the ordering of a sincere man whose purposes bend to God's will and the church's good, not to caprice).

themes: did I use lightness?
do I purpose according to the flesh
yea yea, and nay nay
the charge of fickleness answered

schools.summary: Gill: the false apostles had insinuated that, as Paul had not kept his word in coming as promised, he was not to be depended upon in his ministry — that he might contradict himself and deceive in the one as in the other; wherefore he appeals to God in a very solemn manner, calling him to witness to the truth of his doctrine; Paul affirming, as God is true, that his word toward them was not yea and nay — the appeal that turns from the travel plan to the gospel (Paul, whose detractors used his changed visit to impugn his message, swears by the true God that his preaching, his 'word,' was never a wavering yea-and-nay but steady and trustworthy).

themes: as God is true
our word toward you was not yea and nay
the appeal turning to the gospel
the steady, trustworthy preaching

schools.summary: Gill: Paul, having asserted that the gospel preached was not yea and nay, variable, proceeds to point out the subject of the gospel ministry — the Son of God, Jesus Christ, preached by him, Silvanus (Silas), and Timothy; Paul declaring that the Son of God, Jesus Christ, was not yea and nay but in him was yea — the unchangeableness of Christ the ground of the message's constancy (the very content of their preaching was the immutable Son of God, Hebrews 13:8, in whom there is no wavering or contradiction, so that a message centred on so steadfast a Christ could not itself be fickle).

themes: the Son of God, Jesus Christ... preached among you
by me and Silvanus and Timotheus
was not yea and nay, but in him was yea
the immutable Christ the ground of constancy

schools.summary: Gill: all the promises of God are made in Christ, the Mediator; Barnes: this is why Paul felt bound to the strictest veracity — God always evinces his faithfulness, and as none of his promises fail, Paul felt bound to imitate him; Paul declaring that all the promises of God are in Christ yea, and in him Amen, to God's glory by them — the great affirmation of the certainty of God's promises (every promise of God finds its 'Yea,' its confirmation, substance, and being made good, and its 'Amen,' its sure ratification and certainty, in Christ, who fulfils them all, so that through the apostles' preaching of him God is glorified, none of his promises failing).

debates.question: What does 'all the promises... yea and Amen in him' mean?

sides.position: Every promise of God finds its confirmation ('Yea,' made good and given substance) and its sure ratification ('Amen,' certain) in Christ, who fulfils them all — to God's glory

themes: all the promises of God in him are yea
in him Amen, unto the glory of God
every promise confirmed and ratified in Christ
Christ fulfils all God's promises

schools.summary: Gill: two things are ascribed to God here — first, the establishing of the saints in Christ (that the people of God are in a firm, settled, established condition, encircled in the arms of everlasting love, secure of the favour of God, engraven on his hands and set as a seal on his heart); Paul declaring that it is God who establishes them with the Corinthians in Christ and has anointed them — the divine grounding of both apostles and people (God himself confirms believers in Christ, giving them a settled standing in grace, and anoints his servants with the Spirit's gifts and grace for their work, so the stability of the Christian life and ministry rests on God's own act).

themes: he which stablisheth us with you in Christ
hath anointed us, is God
believers established in Christ
servants anointed with the Spirit

schools.summary: Gill: two more things are attributed to God — the sealing of his people (the use of seals being various: to denote property, to distinguish one thing from another, to show esteem and affection, for security and protection, and to hide and conceal, all expressive of the grace of God in claiming and securing them); Paul declaring that God has also sealed them and given the earnest of the Spirit in their hearts — the double assurance of the Spirit's work (God seals believers, marking them as his own, securing them, stamping his image on them, and gives the Spirit as an 'earnest,' a down-payment and pledge guaranteeing the full inheritance to come, Ephesians 1:14, the indwelling Spirit both the mark of ownership and the foretaste of glory).

themes: who hath also sealed us
the earnest of the Spirit in our hearts
the Spirit the mark of ownership
the Spirit the pledge of the inheritance

schools.summary: Chrysostom notes an apparent contradiction — Paul earlier implied he came not because he purposed not according to the flesh and was led by the authority of the Spirit, but here says it was his own choice, to spare them; yet both are true, the Spirit's leading and his loving forbearance agreeing; Paul calling God to witness upon his soul that it was to spare the Corinthians that he had not yet come to Corinth — the solemn oath revealing his tender motive (Paul, who could have come in severity to punish their faults, stayed away on purpose out of love, to give them time to repent and be spared the rod, the delay a mercy not a slight, his very absence love giving room for repentance).

themes: I call God for a record upon my soul
to spare you I came not as yet unto Corinth
the delay a mercy, not a slight
Paul stayed away out of love

schools.summary: Gill: having spoken of sparing them, lest it be thought he and his fellow ministers assumed any tyrannical power over the churches or lorded it over God's heritage (1 Peter 5:3), Paul subjoins this denial; Paul disclaiming dominion over their faith and professing instead to be a helper of their joy, since by faith they stand — the true character of gospel ministry (the apostles do not lord it over the people's faith, which rests on God alone, by which they stand, but serve as fellow-workers for their joy, the minister a helper not a master of the believer's faith, ministry being service not domination).

themes: not for that we have dominion over your faith
but are helpers of your joy
for by faith ye stand
the minister a helper, not a master
